title: Dyeing with rhubarb leaves
subtitle: Using Rheum rhabarbarum leaves as both mordant and dye source for a yellow-green on wool
excerpt: Rhubarb leaves contain oxalic acid, which acts as both a mordant and a colorant on wool. An overnight pre-soak extracts the oxalic acid and dye compounds; the resulting decoction gives a soft yellow-green on wool without a separate mineral mordant. Oxalic acid is toxic if ingested: gloves and eye protection are mandatory throughout and the leaves must be handled carefully. This tutorial covers the full method including safety precautions and wastewater disposal.
sourceNotes: Hummel, J. J. The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics. London: Cassell, 1885. Public domain. Internet Archive. Oxalic-acid mordant chemistry; action of oxalic acid as a combined mordant and dye-modifier on wool protein fibre.

glossaryTerms.definition: A compound that bonds to both the fibre and the dye molecule, creating a stable bridge between them. Rhubarb leaves are unusual in that oxalic acid, present in the leaf, acts as a mordant alongside functioning as part of the dye complex. No separate mineral mordant is required.

A dye extract made by simmering plant material in water, straining the solids, and using the remaining liquid as the dye bath. Rhubarb leaves are pre-soaked overnight and then simmered to make the decoction.

A class of polyphenolic compounds present in bark, leaves, and fruit skins. Rhubarb leaves contain both oxalic acid and some tannin; together they give the leaf its dual mordanting and dyeing action on protein fibres.

Abbreviated as WOF. The dry weight of the fibre being dyed, used as a baseline for calculating dye material quantities. A recipe using leaves at 200% WOF means 200 g of leaves for every 100 g of dry fibre.

attrs.title: Rhubarb leaf toxicity
attrs.body: Rhubarb leaves contain oxalic acid and are toxic if ingested. Wear gloves and eye protection throughout. Keep away from children, pets, and food preparation areas.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots are dedicated and never return to food use. Wear gloves, an apron, and closed-toe shoes. Work outdoors or with extraction running.

content.text: Rhubarb leaves are one of the few garden dye materials that also act as their own
mordant
. The leaves contain oxalic acid in high concentration alongside some
tannin

. On wool, the oxalic acid binds to the protein fibre and simultaneously acts on the dye compounds, giving a yellow-green without a separate mineral mordant. The result is a soft yellow-green: not a saturated lime, but a grassy yellow with a perceptible green component, something between a straw-yellow and a sage.

The leaves are taken from the rhubarb plant after the stalks have been harvested (the stalks are edible; the leaves are not). Fresh leaves or lightly dried leaves both work. An overnight pre-soak extracts the oxalic acid and dye compounds into the soaking water, reducing the simmering time needed for the
decoction
weight of fibre
(WOF) of leaves: 200 g of leaves for 100 g of dry fibre.
What you need

items.name: Wool yarn or fleece, dry weight
Rhubarb leaves, fresh or lightly dried (200% WOF)
Stainless steel dye-pot, dedicated to dyeing
Fine-mesh sieve or muslin for straining
Kilner jar or container for pre-soak
Safety glasses or goggles for leaf handling

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-soak the leaves overnight

Put on gloves and eye protection before handling the leaves. Tear or roughly chop 200 g of rhubarb leaves and place in the kilner jar or a covered container. Cover completely with cold water. Leave to soak overnight, at least 8 to 12 hours. The soaking water will turn yellow-green as the oxalic acid and dye compounds dissolve. Transfer the leaves and all their soaking liquid to the dye-pot the next morning.
Step 2: Pre-wet the fibre

Submerge the wool in cool water and leave for at least 30 minutes before dyeing. Because rhubarb-leaf dyeing does not use a separate mineral mordant bath, the pre-wetting is the only preparation before the dye bath itself. Squeeze gently without wringing.
Step 3: Extract the dye bath

Bring the pot of soaked leaves and their liquid to 80°C over 20 minutes and hold at 80°C for 30 minutes, pressing the leaves occasionally. The bath will deepen to a stronger yellow-green. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve or muslin, pressing the leaves to extract as much liquid as possible. Discard the spent leaves in a sealed bag into general waste. Do not compost them: the oxalic acid content is high and can affect plants in a compost heap. Return the strained yellow-green liquid to the pot.
Step 4: Dye bath

Lower the pre-wetted wool into the strained dye bath. Bring to 80°C over 20 minutes and hold at 80°C for 60 minutes. Turn the wool every 15 minutes. The oxalic acid in the bath will bond with the wool protein while the dye compounds deposit colour simultaneously. Lift a small section after 45 minutes and rinse under cold water to check the colour: a soft yellow-green is the expected result. Remove the wool once the colour is settled.
Step 5: Rinse and dry

Lift the wool and cool slightly before rinsing. Rinse in water at a similar temperature to avoid felting; cold water straight from the tap onto hot wool will cause shrinkage. Rinse until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing. Dry in shade. The yellow-green is reasonably stable on wool given the oxalic acid mordanting, but will gradually fade in prolonged direct sunlight like most plant-based yellows.
Variations

Rhubarb leaves at 200% WOF give the base yellow-green on wool. Increasing to 300% WOF intensifies the result toward a slightly stronger yellow with more green character. Reducing to 100% WOF gives a paler straw-yellow with less green shift.

Adding 25% WOF alum potash as a conventional mineral mordant before the rhubarb leaf dye bath strengthens the colour and improves light fastness compared with the leaf-only approach. In this case, run a standard mordant bath at 80°C for 45 minutes before lowering the mordanted wool into the rhubarb dye bath. The result is a sharper, more durable yellow-green.

On cotton or linen the results are poor. Oxalic acid bonds to wool protein; it does not bond to cellulose fibres. Cotton and linen need a separate tannin pre-treatment and mineral mordant to take any colour from this dye bath.
Common mistakes

items.problem: Colour is very pale with almost no green component
items.cause: Too few leaves relative to fibre weight, or the extraction did not reach 80°C
items.fix: Use at least 200% WOF of leaves. Hold the extraction at 80°C for 30 minutes, pressing the leaves firmly during straining.

items.problem: Wool has an unpleasant smell after dyeing
items.cause: Normal for rhubarb-leaf work; the oxalic acid has a sharp, slightly acrid smell when hot
items.fix: Work outdoors or with strong extraction. The smell disperses as the wool dries. Rinse thoroughly and dry outside where possible.

items.cause: Wool was not pre-wetted, or was lowered into the bath without temperature equalisation
items.fix: Pre-wet the wool for 30 minutes in cool water. Lower it gently into a warm bath before bringing up to temperature; do not drop cold wool straight into a hot bath.

items.problem: Colour shifts strongly toward yellow with almost no green
items.cause: Water is hard or acidic; acidic conditions suppress the green component
items.fix: Use rainwater or softened water for the dye bath. A very small pinch of soda ash can raise the pH slightly and encourage the green shift without overalkalising the bath.

content.text: Wastewater disposal

The rhubarb-leaf dye bath contains oxalic acid. At home quantities (the bath from 200 g of leaves and 100 g of fibre), cooled spent bath can go down a foul drain with plenty of running water. Do not pour it into a storm drain, a watercourse, a garden soak-away, or onto plants: oxalic acid disrupts soil chemistry and is harmful to aquatic life at higher concentrations.

Spent leaves should go into general waste in a sealed bag rather than food waste or a home compost heap. The residual oxalic acid content is too high for safe composting. For larger quantities of spent bath (over 2 litres), contact your local council waste service.
